caveats: a: No public six-digit occupation matrix for NAICS 713120 was found.
a: Dave & Buster's includes substantial restaurant and other entertainment operations and is much larger than the lens.
a: The frozen labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization adjustment, which may distort the level even though it is not re-estimated here.
rho: Observed labor efficiencies are not isolated to AI and may reflect conventional process changes.
rho: Technology maturity varies sharply between modern card-based centers and legacy cash or token arcades.
rho: Integration and cybersecurity costs can absorb savings at smaller locations.
e: Primary-industry coding can be unstable for mixed entertainment and dining venues.
e: Establishment and firm counts differ for multi-location chains.
e: Some arcades operate under leases or concessions that constrain control transfer.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed deals.
s5: The Main Event acquisition is far above the target EBITDA band.
s5: Location leases, equipment liens, and franchise or licensing terms may impede transfer.
q: The cited gross margin is not an AI-benefit retention measure.
q: Independent arcades may lack national-brand pricing power.
q: Savings may need to fund new games, remodels, or promotions to sustain traffic.
d5: BLS combines theme parks and arcades at four digits.
d5: The public-company comparison is one operator and includes food, beverages, bowling, and other attractions.
d5: New game investment makes constant-quality demand especially difficult to observe.
o: Operator requirement is distinct from the number of on-site staff.
o: Small unattended game rooms may be viable in controlled host locations, but route operations fall outside NAICS 713120.
o: Mixed dining and party venues require more human operation than pure arcades.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily direct-to-consumer pay-per-play or timed admission, increasingly through stored-value RFID cards, kiosks, and mobile applications; redemption prizes, parties, food and beverage, memberships, and promotions provide ancillary and repeat revenue.
lens.excluded_activities: Gambling, billiard and pool parlors, route operators that place or service machines in third-party venues, captive internal arcades without separately transferable operations, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators of amusement arcades and arcade-focused parlors that earn repeat revenue from external guests through game play, stored-value cards, redemptions, parties, memberships or loyalty programs, and related food and beverage.

a.bridge: BLS reports the occupation mix for combined NAICS 713100 amusement parks and arcades, not six-digit arcade wage weights or AI exposure. O*NET describes attendant tasks across amusement and recreation employers, while a large eat-and-play operator shows actual arcade workflows. I map wage-bearing tasks in guest information, parties and bookings, marketing, loyalty, pricing, scheduling, records, finance, and management to partial AI exposure, with little exposure assigned to cleaning, food service, machine repair, stocking, and in-person service recovery. Theme parks likely have more safety and ride-operating labor than arcades, so the combined BLS mix may understate arcade digital-task share; however, existing arcade kiosks and stored-value systems mean it may overstate tasks that remain not-yet-automated.
caveats: No public six-digit occupation matrix for NAICS 713120 was found.
Dave & Buster's includes substantial restaurant and other entertainment operations and is much larger than the lens.
The frozen labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization adjustment, which may distort the level even though it is not re-estimated here.
a.change_evidence: Collect payroll-by-role and time-on-task data from 15-20 standalone arcades and arcade-led family entertainment centers, explicitly separating work already removed by kiosks and card systems from current guest service, parties, marketing, scheduling, repair, cleaning, and food tasks.
a.rationale: The combined industry employs substantial attendants, cashiers, office support, customer-service, sales, and management staff. Arcade transactions are already highly self-served through cards and kiosks, so that work is not counted again. Remaining exposed work includes routine guest communications, group-event sales support, targeted marketing, loyalty offers, staff scheduling, demand forecasting, pricing analysis, reporting, reconciliation, and parts of management administration. Physical machine uptime, redemption inventory, food service, cleaning, security, and on-floor customer assistance remain labor-intensive.

d5.bridge: BLS real output covers combined amusement parks and arcades, while Dave & Buster's comparable sales cover a large mixed entertainment-and-dining chain and include price, mix, and food effects. I use the combined industry's 2024 real-output decline and the chain's 2025 same-store decline as downside evidence, balanced by continuing new-site investment and differentiation through social games that are hard to reproduce at home. Theme parks dominate parts of the BLS series, and nominal comparable sales do not equal constant-quality arcade visits, so the range is wide.
caveats: BLS combines theme parks and arcades at four digits.
The public-company comparison is one operator and includes food, beverages, bowling, and other attractions.
New game investment makes constant-quality demand especially difficult to observe.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain five years of same-location plays, unique guests, party bookings, active loyalty users, and constant-price sales from a representative independent-arcade panel, separated from food, new sites, remodels, and game-quality upgrades.
d5.rationale: Combined amusement-park and arcade real output declined 3.2% in 2024 after the post-pandemic recovery. Dave & Buster's fiscal 2025 revenue fell 1.4% and comparable-store sales fell 5.0%, primarily from lower walk-in business, even as it opened 11 venues. Demand faces intense competition from home gaming, streaming, restaurants, and other attractions, but multiplayer, large-format, redemption, party, and social experiences retain out-of-home differentiation. The central five-year quantity view is therefore approximately flat.

caveats: Primary-industry coding can be unstable for mixed entertainment and dining venues.
Establishment and firm counts differ for multi-location chains.
Some arcades operate under leases or concessions that constrain control transfer.
e.change_evidence: Classify all 64 supplied in-band firms using business descriptions, revenue mix, legal ownership, number of sites, standalone financials, captive status, and owner dependence from Data Axle, PitchBook, lender files, and operator interviews.
e.rationale: The Census definition is narrow and coherent: operators of nongambling amusement arcades and parlors. Most LMM firms should therefore be repeat-revenue venue operators rather than suppliers. Exclusions remain for captive mall, restaurant, cinema, or resort units lacking standalone control; businesses whose primary activity is food, bowling, or other attractions despite an arcade component; noncommercial operators; and heavily owner-dependent single sites. The supplied LMM firm count is accepted without re-estimation.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 713120 ($823,588,663) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 713120 ($5,530,818,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1664 (margins.json[71]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Recreation EBITDA/Sales 16.64%); revenue/employee $90,002 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 713120); class <5: 1,352 firms, avg revenue/firm $294,530 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $49,010 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 514 firms, avg revenue/firm $518,230 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $86,233 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 447 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,032,553 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $171,817 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 473 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,808,414 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $467,320 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 64 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,694,672 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,446,793 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $179,989,571 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,950,265 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 64

caveats: Operator requirement is distinct from the number of on-site staff.
Small unattended game rooms may be viable in controlled host locations, but route operations fall outside NAICS 713120.
Mixed dining and party venues require more human operation than pure arcades.
o.change_evidence: Study unattended arcade formats and remote-operations pilots, including machine uptime, shrink, chargebacks, prize fulfillment, incident rates, landlord requirements, and the share of visits that require staff intervention.
o.rationale: Game play is already largely self-service, yet the current service basket still depends on a venue operator to select and finance games, run payments and redemptions, maintain uptime, stock prizes, clean the location, host parties, manage food and beverage, resolve guest problems, and comply with leases and local rules. Fully unattended micro-arcades could remove some operator labor, but not the accountable business operating the venue; displacement to home gaming is treated in d5 rather than here.

q.bridge: The public filing measures pricing, revenue mix, variable costs, and promotions at a large mixed entertainment-and-dining chain, not five-year retention of AI benefits at LMM arcades. I infer retention from direct pay-per-play sales with operator-controlled chip pricing, ticket payouts, prize pricing, promotions, and loyalty offers. The chain's brand and scale may give more pricing power than independent arcades, biasing the base upward; local fixed-price consumer sales still imply little automatic contractual pass-through.
caveats: The cited gross margin is not an AI-benefit retention measure.
Independent arcades may lack national-brand pricing power.
Savings may need to fund new games, remodels, or promotions to sustain traffic.
q.change_evidence: Analyze game-price and chip-package histories, discount depth, prize-cost ratios, party pricing, customer cohorts, local competitor openings, and contribution margins before and after automation at independent arcades.
q.rationale: Arcades sell directly to guests rather than under hourly or cost-plus contracts. Dave & Buster's reports that entertainment was 62.9% of fiscal 2025 revenue and carried a 91.9% gross margin, while management actively changes game pricing, discounts, promotions, ticket payouts, and redemption-center pricing. These levers permit substantial retention of implemented savings, but local competition, affordability, loyalty rewards, guest expectations, and reinvestment in new games will share some benefit.

rho.bridge: A large public operator documents operational technology, kiosks, loyalty data, workforce management, machine-monitoring applications, and labor efficiencies but does not measure five-year AI implementation in LMM arcades. I infer a majority implementation share for the exposed digital workflows because the transaction and operating data infrastructure already exists. Large-chain integration resources bias the evidence upward; simpler LMM workflows and packaged vendors partly offset that bias.
caveats: Observed labor efficiencies are not isolated to AI and may reflect conventional process changes.
Technology maturity varies sharply between modern card-based centers and legacy cash or token arcades.
Integration and cybersecurity costs can absorb savings at smaller locations.
rho.change_evidence: Review vendor adoption and pre/post labor hours for Embed, Intercard, Sacoa or comparable payment systems plus CRM, party-booking, workforce, and maintenance tools across independent arcades; interview managers about overrides, errors, and guest escalation rates.
rho.rationale: Existing card, kiosk, mobile, CRM, workforce, and game-monitoring infrastructure lowers the data and workflow barrier for AI-assisted marketing, customer support, party booking, pricing, scheduling, maintenance triage, and reporting. Implementation remains incomplete because small operators may lack clean integrations, management attention, or reliable volume; guest-facing exceptions, prize disputes, machine failures, food service, and safety still require people.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans for employer-business owners across all US industries, not completed qualifying control transfers among LMM arcades. Its 22% plan rate is discounted for noncompletion, closures, family gifts without outside control, and lease or concept-transfer friction, while observed acquisition of a 50-location entertainment-center operator confirms transferability. Stated intentions likely exceed completed qualifying transfers; the large observed transaction may overstate liquidity for single-site arcades.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed deals.
The Main Event acquisition is far above the target EBITDA band.
Location leases, equipment liens, and franchise or licensing terms may impede transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Create a ten-year transaction and closure census for US arcades and arcade-led family entertainment centers using PitchBook, CapIQ, BizBuySell, state entity records, landlord notices, and industry press, matched to pre-deal size and control outcome.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years and 52.3% of employer businesses were owned by people age 55 or older. Arcade operations can transfer through entity, asset, or multi-site concept sales, as demonstrated by Dave & Buster's completed acquisition of Main Event. A high-teens central probability discounts survey intentions and excludes closures, property-only transactions, and internal reorganizations.

principal_driver: Digitized direct-consumer operations and high-margin game play create practical AI-enabled workflow opportunities with limited contractual pass-through.
principal_weakness: The simplest arcade transaction is already self-service, while demand is discretionary and exposed to home gaming and continual reinvestment requirements.

sources.supports: Reports 227,040 combined-industry workers and detailed employment shares and wages, including amusement attendants at 30.66%, cashiers at 6.61%, office and administrative support at 4.50%, management at 3.40%, and customer-service representatives at 1.09%.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 713100 Amusement Parks and Arcades

sources.supports: Lists ticket and fee collection, guest information, records, inventory, cleaning, equipment inspection, refreshments, scheduling, and operation of amusement equipment among attendant tasks.
sources.title: O*NET: Amusement and Recreation Attendants, 39-3091.00

sources.supports: Reports RFID/card game activation through kiosks, mobile apps, or staff; 62.9% entertainment revenue and 91.9% entertainment gross margin; pricing, loyalty, technology and self-service initiatives; 11 venue openings; a 5.0% comparable-sales decline; and competition from home gaming.
sources.title: Dave & Buster's Entertainment Fiscal 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Documents a loyalty program with nearly six million members, real-time workforce management, and a proprietary mobile-supported system that identifies game issues and needed repairs.
sources.title: Dave & Buster's Entertainment Fiscal 2023 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Finds AI use highly uneven across tasks and occupations; November 2025 Claude.ai use was 52% augmentation and 45% automation, with computer and mathematical tasks disproportionately represented.
sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index: New building blocks for understanding AI use

sources.supports: Defines NAICS 713120 as establishments operating nongambling amusement arcades and parlors and excludes route operators that supply or service devices in others' establishments, billiard parlors, and gambling operations.
sources.title: US Census Bureau NAICS Sector 71 Definitions

sources.supports: Reports that 52.3% of US employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Confirms the June 29, 2022 completed control acquisition of entertainment-and-dining venue operator Main Event after shareholder and regulatory approvals.
sources.title: Dave & Buster's Completes Acquisition of Main Event

sources.supports: BLS Industry Productivity data show combined NAICS 7131 real sectoral output rose 0.5% in 2023 and declined 3.2% in 2024, following a large post-pandemic rebound.
sources.title: Real Sectoral Output for Amusement Parks and Arcades, Percent Change from Year Ago

title: Amusement Arcades
